text: The surgeon approaches the thymoma from the left side using thoracoscopy, with the patient positioned supine. Three ports are utilized: two for surgical instruments and one for the camera, with an optional fourth port for tissue retraction.

The phrenic nerve is visualized alongside the thymic tumor. Carbon dioxide gas is used to insufflate the thoracic cavity at a pressure of nine to ten millimeters of mercury, aiding in the visualization of the surgical field.
The surgeon begins adhesiolysis to separate adhesions between the thymic tumor and the lung, carefully preserving the phrenic nerve to maintain diaphragm functionality.
The surgeon carefully dissects the parietal pleura over the mediastinum, located just anterior to the phrenic nerve, using cold techniques to prevent thermal injury to the nerve.
The surgeon makes an anterior cut along the anterior reflection of the pleura, causing the anterior mediastinal contents to drop down towards the specimen.

The surgeon carefully secures the thymic vessels, with particular attention to the thin veins that drain into larger veins such as the brachiocephalic vein, to prevent tearing and potential catastrophic bleeding.
The innominate vein is visible beneath the previously ligated vessel, with surrounding anatomical structures clearly exposed.
The surgeon carefully dissects the phrenic nerve away from the tumor, ensuring it remains free from invasion. The nerve is visibly separated from the tumor, preserving its integrity.

The surgeon dissects the entire pericardial tissue along with the tumor, incorporating it into the specimen. The right pleura is visible, and the formation of the superior vena cava is observed as the dissection progresses.

The left horn of the thymus is visible, with the innominate vein clearly defined. The surgeon carefully dissects the thymic horns from the neck, ensuring to ligate the superior thymic vessels to prevent bleeding.
The surgeon dissects and reflects the entire anterior mediastinal fat, removing it along with the thymectomy specimen.
The posterior thymic vessels, which are direct branches from the arch of the aorta, are visible in the surgical field.
The surgical field shows the floor of the thymus after dissection, with the arch of the aorta and the pulmonary trunk visible. The phrenic nerve is also present in the field.
The surgeon extracts the thymectomy specimen through a sub-xiphoid incision, using a wound protector to minimize tissue trauma. Bilateral chest drains are placed following the extraction.
